cli/internal/agent/testdata/reviewer/reviewer-stream-oversized-body.jsonl:1:{"id":"as-bc4b0e90","timestamp":"2026-04-15T01:50:16.151938804Z","harness":"claude","surface":"claude","canonical_target":"claude-sonnet-4-6","model":"claude-sonnet-4-6","prompt_len":13868,"prompt":"\u003cexecute-bead\u003e\n  \u003cbead id=\"axon-dccbdf73\"\u003e\n    \u003ctitle\u003eauth-jwt-pipeline: sign + verify with revocation cache + request extension\u003c/title\u003e\n    \u003cdescription\u003e\nImplement the full JWT verification middleware in axon-server.\n\n## What you're building\n\nAn axum middleware layer that runs on every data-plane request. It validates the JWT, installs a (user_id, tenant_id, grants) tuple into the request extension, and returns a typed AuthError on any failure. Downstream handlers read the extension and trust it completely.\n\n## Scope — exactly what to add\n\n### New file: `crates/axon-server/src/auth_pipeline.rs`\n\nA module exposing two things:\n\n1. A `JwtIssuer` helper that signs a `JwtClaims` into a compact JWT string. HS256 is the only algorithm required for this bead. The signing key is a `Vec\u003cu8\u003e` passed in at construction. Provide `issue(\u0026self, claims: \u0026JwtClaims) -\u003e Result\u003cString, AuthError\u003e` and a `verify(\u0026self, token: \u0026str) -\u003e Result\u003cJwtClaims, AuthError\u003e` for round-tripping in tests.\n\n2. An axum middleware `async fn jwt_verify_layer` that runs the 8-step verification order below. On success, installs a `ResolvedIdentity { user_id, tenant_id, grants }` into the request extension. On failure, returns a response built via `AuthError::into_response()` so the mapping to HTTP status + error.code is consistent.\n\nThe middleware also owns an `InMemoryRevocationCache` (a small `tokio::sync::RwLock\u003cHashSet\u003cUuid\u003e\u003e` is fine; nothing fancy) and falls back to SQL (via the storage adapter's new `is_jti_revoked` method) on cache miss.\n\n### Extend: `crates/axon-core/src/auth.rs`\n\n- Add `ResolvedIdentity { pub user_id: UserId, pub tenant_id: TenantId, pub grants: Grants }`.\n- Add `impl AuthError` with `pub fn status_code(\u0026self) -\u003e u16` returning 401 or 403 per ADR-018 §4 failure table, and `pub fn error_code(\u0026self) -\u003e \u0026'static str` returning the stable code string. (An axum `IntoResponse` impl can live in axon-server.)\n- Add `impl Grants` with `pub fn find_database(\u0026self, name: \u0026str) -\u003e Option\u003c\u0026GrantedDatabase\u003e` and `impl GrantedDatabase` with `pub fn has_op(\u0026self, op: Op) -\u003e bool`.\n\n### Extend: `crates/axon-storage/src/adapter.rs` (trait)\n\nAdd one method to the `StorageAdapter` trait:\n\n```rust\nasync fn is_jti_revoked(\u0026self, jti: Uuid) -\u003e Result\u003cbool, AxonError\u003e;\n```\n\nImplement it in `crates/axon-storage/src/memory.rs` (store a `HashSet\u003cUuid\u003e`; default-empty), `crates/axon-storage/src/sqlite.rs`, and `crates/axon-storage/src/postgres.rs` (`SELECT 1 FROM credential_revocations WHERE jti = $1`). The A1 schema (`auth_schema.rs`) already has the `credential_revocations` table — just query it.\n\n### Wire into: `crates/axon-server/src/lib.rs`\n\nRe-export `auth_pipeline::{jwt_verify_layer, JwtIssuer, InMemoryRevocationCache}`. Don't wire it into the main router yet — that's D-phase work. For this bead, the middleware just needs to exist and be testable in isolation.\n\n## Verification order (normative — ADR-018 §4)\n\n1. Extract `Authorization: Bearer \u003cjwt\u003e` from the request. Missing header → `AuthError::Unauthenticated` (401). The `--no-auth` bypass is NOT part of this bead — that's E2.\n2. Parse and verify the JWT signature with HS256 against the issuer's key. Signature invalid → `CredentialInvalid` (401). Malformed structure → `CredentialMalformed` (401). `aud` as a JSON array → `CredentialMalformed` (this falls out of the A2 `parse_claims` wrapper — use it).\n3. Check `nbf` and `exp` against `SystemTime::now()` with a 30s skew budget. `exp` more than 30s in the past → `CredentialExpired` (401). `nbf` more than 30s in the future → `CredentialNotYetValid` (401).\n4. Check `claims.jti` against the revocation cache, then SQL on miss. Hit → `CredentialRevoked` (401). Populate the cache on SQL hit to speed up subsequent checks.\n5. Compare `claims.aud` to the `{tenant}` segment extracted from the URL path. Mismatch → `CredentialWrongTenant` (**403**, not 401 — see the table). The URL extraction path is: look at `request.uri().path()` and parse the leading `/tenants/{tenant}/databases/{database}/...` segments. For this bead, implement a small helper `fn extract_tenant_database(path: \u0026str) -\u003e Option\u003c(\u0026str, \u0026str)\u003e` that returns `None` for non-data-plane paths so they skip the middleware.\n6. Look up the `sub` UUID in the `users` table via a new `StorageAdapter::get_user(UserId) -\u003e Result\u003cOption\u003cUser\u003e, AxonError\u003e` method. User not found or `user.suspended_at_ms.is_some()` → `UserSuspended` (401).\n7. Check tenant membership: a new `StorageAdapter::get_tenant_member(TenantId, UserId) -\u003e Result\u003cOption\u003cTenantMember\u003e, AxonError\u003e`. Missing → `NotATenantMember` (403).\n8. Walk `claims.grants.databases[]` looking for an entry matching the URL `{database}`. None → `DatabaseNotGranted` (403). Found → compute the required op from the HTTP method (GET = Read, POST/PUT/PATCH/DELETE = Write) and intersect with the grant's ops. Empty intersection → `OpNotGranted` (403). Admin-requiring routes are out of scope for this bead — the middleware only handles Read/Write classification.\n9. Build `ResolvedIdentity` and install it via `request.extensions_mut().insert(resolved)`.\n\n## Error mapping — must exactly match ADR-018 §4\n\nThe mapping table is copied here verbatim. Do not invent variants; the 14 AuthError variants already exist in axon-core.\n\n| Failure | Status | `error.code` |\n|---|---|---|\n| No Authorization header | 401 | `unauthenticated` |\n| Header not `Bearer \u003ctoken\u003e` or base64 broken | 401 | `credential_malformed` |\n| JWT structurally invalid / missing claims | 401 | `credential_malformed` |\n| `aud` as JSON array | 401 | `credential_malformed` |\n| Signature invalid | 401 | `credential_invalid` |\n| `exp` in past (beyond skew) | 401 | `credential_expired` |\n| `nbf` in future (beyond skew) | 401 | `credential_not_yet_valid` |\n| `jti` revoked | 401 | `credential_revoked` |\n| `iss` unknown | 401 | `credential_foreign_issuer` — for this bead, reject if `claims.iss != issuer.issuer_id` |\n| `aud` ≠ URL tenant | **403** | `credential_wrong_tenant` |\n| `sub` suspended/deleted | 401 | `user_suspended` |\n| `sub` not a tenant member | **403** | `not_a_tenant_member` |\n| URL database not in grants | **403** | `database_not_granted` |\n| Op not in grant | **403** | `op_not_granted` |\n\nNote the 401 vs 403 split: credential-level problems are 401, scope-level problems are 403.\n\n## Testing — all must pass for this bead to be complete\n\n### Unit tests in `crates/axon-server/tests/auth_pipeline_test.rs`\n\nFor every row of the error mapping table, build the minimal defective request and assert the middleware produces exactly that `(status, error_code)` pair. Table-driven with `#[test]` or a single `#[test]` that iterates an array of cases.\n\nAdditionally:\n\n- `jwt_roundtrip_succeeds`: issue a valid JWT with `JwtIssuer::issue`, verify it with `JwtIssuer::verify`, assert claims match.\n- `valid_jwt_populates_request_extension`: run the full middleware on a valid JWT, assert the response is 200 and the handler sees `ResolvedIdentity` in extensions.\n- `revocation_takes_effect_within_one_second`: issue a valid JWT, verify it succeeds, insert the jti into the revocation cache, verify the next request returns 401 `credential_revoked`. Do NOT use sleep — invalidate the cache directly.\n- `clock_skew_30s_accepted_31s_rejected`: construct a JWT with `exp` at `now - 29s` (should succeed) and one with `exp` at `now - 31s` (should fail with `credential_expired`). Do the same for `nbf`.\n- `ops_matrix`: for each HTTP method in {GET, POST, PUT, PATCH, DELETE} and each op set in the grant, assert the expected 200/403 outcome.\n\n### Integration test in `crates/axon-server/tests/auth_pipeline_integration_test.rs`\n\nBuild a minimal axum router with one dummy route `GET /tenants/acme/databases/orders/ping`. Install the middleware. Hit it with:\n- No auth header → 401 `unauthenticated`\n- Valid JWT with `grants: [{name: \"orders\", ops: [read]}]` → 200\n- Valid JWT with the wrong tenant in aud → 403 `credential_wrong_tenant`\n- Valid JWT with grants for a different database → 403 `database_not_granted`\n\n### axon-sim workload `inv_018_grant_enforcement`\n\n**If this is too large for one pass, skip the axon-sim workload and note it in the commit message for a follow-up bead.** The core verification must ship; the simulation workload can follow. Do not block the whole bead on the workload.\n\n## Crate dependencies you will need\n\nAdd to `crates/axon-server/Cargo.toml` under `[dependencies]`:\n```toml\njsonwebtoken = \"9\"\n```\n\nEverything else (axum, tower, tokio, uuid, serde_json, http) is already in the workspace.\n\n## Context you must know about\n\n### A1 landed: schema\n- `crates/axon-storage/src/auth_schema.rs` has the SQL for `users`, `user_identities`, `tenant_users`, `credential_revocations`, etc.\n- `user_identities.external_id` (NOT `subject`) — already corrected.\n- Migration runs via `apply_auth_migrations_sqlite` / `_postgres`.\n\n### A2 landed: types\n- `crates/axon-core/src/auth.rs` has all the types: `TenantId`, `UserId`, `User`, `UserIdentity`, `Tenant`, `TenantMember`, `TenantRole`, `Op`, `GrantedDatabase`, `Grants`, `JwtClaims`, `AuthError` (14 variants).\n- `parse_claims(\u0026str) -\u003e Result\u003cJwtClaims, AuthError\u003e` wraps serde_json and maps errors to `CredentialMalformed`. USE IT.\n- `AUTH_ERROR_VARIANT_COUNT == 14` is enforced via compile-time exhaustive match.\n- Delegation helper on `TenantRole`: `admin.can_delegate(op)` / etc.\n\n### Files you should NOT touch\n- Anything outside `crates/axon-server/src/auth_pipeline.rs`, `crates/axon-core/src/auth.rs` (additive), `crates/axon-storage/src/{adapter,memory,sqlite,postgres}.rs` (additive), and the two new test files.\n- The existing `crates/axon-server/src/auth.rs` — that's the Tailscale whois path. Leave it alone. Your pipeline is a new module.\n\n## Quality bar\n\nYou are the implementation step. A post-merge review with opus will verify the result against the AC. Adds tables for:\\n4\\t//! - tenants: global account boundary\\n5\\t//! - users: global user identities\\n6\\t//! - user_identities: federated identity mapping\\n7\\t//! - tenant_users: M:N tenant membership\\n8\\t//! - tenant_databases: tenant-to-database mapping\\n9\\t//! - credential_revocations: revoked JWTs\\n10\\t//!\\n11\\t//! Note: `audit_retention_policies` is intentionally NOT included here — it\\n12\\t//! belongs to F1 audit-attribution (a separate bead / feature).\\n13\\t\\n14\\t/// Apply auth/tenancy migrations to a SQLite connection.\\n15\\t///\\n16\\t/// This is idempotent — running it multiple times on the same database\\n17\\t/// produces the same result without errors or duplicate rows.\\n18\\tpub fn apply_auth_migrations_sqlite(conn: \u0026rusqlite::Connection) -\u003e Result\u003c(), String\u003e {\\n19\\t    // Create tenants table.\\n20\\t    // UNIQUE(name) enforced by the UNIQUE constraint on the name column.\\n21\\t    conn.execute_batch(\\n22\\t        \\\"CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS tenants (\\n23\\t            id                 TEXT PRIMARY KEY,\\n24\\t            name               TEXT NOT NULL UNIQUE,\\n25\\t            display_name       TEXT NOT NULL,\\n26\\t            created_at_ms      INTEGER NOT NULL,\\n27\\t            updated_at_ms      INTEGER NOT NULL\\n28\\t        )\\\",\\n29\\t    )\\n30\\t    .map_err(|e| format!(\\\"tenants table: {e}\\\"))?;\\n31\\t\\n32\\t    // Create users table.\\n33\\t    conn.execute_batch(\\n34\\t        \\\"CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS users (\\n35\\t            id                 TEXT PRIMARY KEY,\\n36\\t            display_name       TEXT NOT NULL,\\n37\\t            email              TEXT,\\n38\\t            created_at_ms      INTEGER NOT NULL,\\n39\\t            suspended_at_ms    INTEGER\\n40\\t        )\\\",\\n41\\t    )\\n42\\t    .map_err(|e| format!(\\\"users table: {e}\\\"))?;\\n43\\t\\n44\\t    // Create user_identities table.\\n45\\t    // PRIMARY KEY (provider, external_id) enforces UNIQUE(provider, external_id).\\n46\\t    conn.execute_batch(\\n47\\t        \\\"CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS user_identities (\\n48\\t            provider       TEXT NOT NULL,\\n49\\t            external_id    TEXT NOT NULL,\\n50\\t            user_id        TEXT NOT NULL REFERENCES users(id),\\n51\\t            created_at_ms  INTEGER NOT NULL,\\n52\\t            PRIMARY KEY (provider, external_id)\\n53\\t        )\\\",\\n54\\t    )\\n55\\t    .map_err(|e| format!(\\\"user_identities table: {e}\\\"))?;\\n56\\t\\n57\\t    // Create tenant_users table.\\n58\\t    // PRIMARY KEY (tenant_id, user_id) is the composite PK required by ADR-018.\\n59\\t    conn.execute_batch(\\n60\\t        \\\"CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS tenant_users (\\n61\\t            tenant_id    TEXT NOT NULL REFERENCES tenants(id),\\n62\\t            user_id      TEXT NOT NULL REFERENCES users(id),\\n63\\t            role         TEXT NOT NULL,\\n64\\t            added_at_ms  INTEGER NOT NULL,\\n65\\t            PRIMARY KEY (tenant_id, user_id)\\n66\\t        )\\\",\\n67\\t    )\\n68\\t    .map_err(|e| format!(\\\"tenant_users table: {e}\\\"))?;\\n69\\t\\n70\\t    // Create tenant_databases table.\\n71\\t    conn.execute_batch(\\n72\\t        \\\"CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS tenant_databases (\\n73\\t Axon server — HTTP gateway, gRPC service, MCP stdio, and authentication.\\n2\\t//!\\n3\\t//! # Authentication\\n4\\t//!\\n5\\t//! Identity is resolved once per request by [`auth::AuthContext`] and\\n6\\t//! injected as a typed extension.  Three modes are supported:\\n7\\t//!\\n8\\t//! | Mode | Flag | Actor | Role |\\n9\\t//! |------|------|-------|------|\\n10\\t//! | `NoAuth` | `--no-auth` | `\\\"anonymous\\\"` | Admin |\\n11\\t//! | `Tailscale` | *(default)* | node name | from ACL tags |\\n12\\t//! | `Guest` | `--guest-role \u003crole\u003e` | `\\\"guest\\\"` | configured role |\\n13\\t//!\\n14\\t//! In `Tailscale` mode the server contacts the local Tailscale daemon over its\\n15\\t//! Unix socket (`/run/tailscale/tailscaled.sock` by default) and calls\\n16\\t//! `/localapi/v0/whois?addr=\u003cpeer\u003e` to resolve the connecting node's identity.\\n17\\t//! Resolved identities are cached by peer IP for the duration of\\n18\\t//! `--auth-cache-ttl-secs` (default 60 s).\\n19\\t//!\\n20\\t//! ACL tag → role mapping:\\n21\\t//! - `tag:axon-admin` → [`Role::Admin`]\\n22\\t//! - `tag:axon-write` / `tag:axon-agent` → [`Role::Write`]\\n23\\t//! - `tag:axon-read` → [`Role::Read`]\\n24\\t//! - no matching tag → `--tailscale-default-role` (default `read`)\\n25\\t//!\\n26\\t//! Connections that are not on the tailnet are rejected with HTTP 401 / gRPC\\n27\\t//! `UNAUTHENTICATED`.  If the Tailscale daemon is unreachable the server\\n28\\t//! returns HTTP 503 / gRPC `UNAVAILABLE`.\\n29\\t//!\\n30\\t//! # Request flow\\n31\\t//!\\n32\\t//! ```text\\n33\\t//! TCP accept\\n34\\t//!   └─ authenticate_http_request  (gateway.rs middleware)\\n35\\t//!        ├─ AuthContext::resolve_peer  →  Identity\\n36\\t//!        └─ insert Identity into request extensions\\n37\\t//!             └─ route handler\\n38\\t//!                  ├─ extract Extension\u003cIdentity\u003e\\n39\\t//!                  ├─ identity.require_read() / require_write() / require_admin()\\n40\\t//!                  └─ handler logic\\n41\\t//! ```\\n42\\t//!\\n43\\t//! gRPC follows the same pattern via [`service::AxonServiceImpl::authorize`].\\n44\\tpub mod .ddx/executions/20260430T005604-c425377f/prompt.md:5:## Two-layer problem

### Layer 1 (mechanical bug)

When a worker invokes `ddx bead create` from inside its execute-bead worktree, the resulting bead IDs are malformed. Reproduced today on this project:

  /home/erik/.local/bin/ddx bead ready  → 11 entries with IDs like
  `.execute-bead-wt-ddx-44236615-20260429T185004-ffa9adf1-e630aeae`

These were the read-coverage child beads spawned from the ddx-44236615 worker (commit ebeb2464). They have valid descriptions, AC, priorities, and labels — only the ID field is mangled. The pattern `.execute-bead-wt-<parent>-<timestamp>-<short>-<random>` matches the worktree directory name format used by `ddx agent execute-bead`. Most likely failure path: the bead-id resolver consults `os.Getwd()` (or equivalent) to derive a project context prefix, sees the worktree dir, and uses it instead of the real project root + a fresh `ddx-<8hex>` short hash.

Concrete impact:
- 50+ char IDs are unwieldy in CLI commands (`ddx bead show <id>`, `ddx bead dep add ...`).
- Cross-project readers and the bead store assume `ddx-<8hex>` prefix matching for tooling (filtering, GraphQL adapters, MCP responses) — these long IDs may sort or filter unexpectedly.
- The IDs encode ephemeral attempt timestamps, which leaks worker-internal state into a permanent identifier.
- 12 such beads exist in the tracker as of this filing (grep `.execute-bead-wt-` in `.ddx/beads.jsonl`).

### Layer 2 (design question — separate decision)

Even if the IDs were correctly formed `ddx-<8hex>`, it is not obvious that `ddx bead create` from inside an execute-bead worker is the right pattern at all. Two alternatives the design has not articulated a position on:

**(a) Append to the parent bead.** When the worker discovers sub-tasks, it appends them as structured items in the parent's description, notes, or a new `kind:discovered-subtask` event. The parent bead becomes the durable record of "what this run found"; future runs (or the operator) can decompose if/when the work warrants its own tracker entries.

**(b) Surface via the execute-bead result.** The agent emits a structured `discovered_subtasks: [...]` field on result.json; the loop / operator decides whether to file new beads. Keeps tracker mutations gated by an explicit decision point.

**(c) Create new beads in-band (current behavior).** The worker calls `ddx bead create` directly. Pro: fastest decomposition. Con: sub-tasks land in the queue without operator review; can flood the queue if the agent over-decomposes; complicates "what was THIS run's contribution" auditing.

Today's behavior is (c) but undocumented. The 11 read-coverage children landing as a single agent's decomposition is exactly the failure mode (c) makes easy: one analysis bead spawned 11 implementation beads in a single run, all P0, all without operator review. The work may be valid — but the pattern needs a position.

## In-scope outcomes

This bead's job is to surface both layers; the implementation work decomposes into follow-ups.

1. **Concrete fix for layer 1**: bead-id resolver should normalize worktree paths to the real project root before allocating an ID. Test: `ddx bead create` invoked with cwd=`<repo>/.execute-bead-wt-...` produces `ddx-<8hex>`, not `.execute-bead-wt-...-<random>`.
2. **Mass repair for the 12 existing malformed beads**: either rewrite their IDs to fresh `ddx-<8hex>` (with a redirect or alias map for any external references), or close them as deferred and re-file with proper IDs. Decision deferred until layer 2 is settled.
3. **Layer 2 decision**: file a separate design bead (or amendment to FEAT-006 / FEAT-010) that picks (a), (b), or (c) and documents the rationale. This bead does NOT pre-decide; it captures the question.

## In-scope files (layer 1 fix)

- `cli/cmd/bead_create.go` (or wherever bead-create resolves project context).
- `cli/internal/bead/store.go` or `cli/internal/bead/id.go` if ID generation lives there.
- A unit test that drops cwd into a worktree-shaped dir and asserts the resulting bead id matches `^ddx-[0-9a-f]{8}$`.

(Locate via: `grep -rn "execute-bead-wt\|FindProjectRoot\|projectRoot" cli/internal/bead/ cli/cmd/bead_*.go`.)

## Out of scope

- The 11 read-coverage child beads' actual implementation work — that's separate beads.
- Default behavior change for whether workers can/should create beads — that's the layer-2 design bead.
- GraphQL or MCP changes to handle long IDs — fix the source instead.

## Acceptance

Layer-1 only (this bead):

1. `cd cli && go test ./cmd/... ./internal/bead/...` passes after the fix.
2. New unit test in `cli/internal/bead/` (or `cli/cmd/`) that simulates a bead-create call from `cwd=<project>/.execute-bead-wt-ddx-XXXXX-<timestamp>-<random>` and asserts the new bead's `id` field matches `^ddx-[0-9a-f]{8}$`. Test fails before the fix, passes after.
3. `grep -E '"id":"\.execute-bead-wt-' .ddx/beads.jsonl` returns either 0 (mass repair) or 12 with a recorded decision in this bead's notes that the existing 12 are deferred to a follow-up bead.
4. A separate bead is filed (or referenced existing one updated) that captures the layer-2 design question (a/b/c). Reference its ID in this bead's events.

## Why one bead, not an epic

Layer 1 is a tight 1-3 file fix. Layer 2 is a design discussion that should be its own artifact, not a sibling task. Splitting them keeps each bounded.

Layer-2 bead and deferral note are acceptable.

Required edits to docs/helix/01-frame/features/FEAT-015-installation-architecture.md:
- Overview bullets: rewrite to describe project-local copy semantics. Remove the 'ddx install --global' bullet entirely.
- New 'Cross-platform invariant' section: ddx creates no symlinks during install; reasoning = Windows compat; plugin source symlinks (in tarballs) are NOT materialized — author's responsibility.
- New 'Migration' section: ddx update --force heals pre-existing projects with home-symlinked skills; stale ~/.ddx/installed.yaml entries are no-op uninstalls; ~/.ddx/plugins/ left in place as inert (user can rm -rf).
- FEAT-011, FEAT-009 cross-references: minimal edits, point readers at FEAT-015 amendment.

Locked decisions to encode in spec:
1. Drop global skills targets (~/.agents/skills/, ~/.claude/skills/).
2. ddx init/install only touch <projectRoot>.
3. Stale ~/.ddx/installed.yaml entries no-op.
4. <projectRoot>/.ddx/plugins/, .agents/skills/, .claude/skills/ are committed.
5. Zero symlinks created by ddx install.
6. ddx install --global removed.
7. ~-prefixed Root.Target manifests rejected with non-zero exit.

.ddx/executions/20260429T065203-5c940767/prompt.md:5:## Status after codex review (2026-04-22)

Original plan was consolidated in this bead but **reduced in scope** after codex's review surfaced that most of the compatibility and data-model work belongs upstream in ddx-agent. This bead is now the **DDx-side tracker only**. Upstream work is queued as separate beads in `~/Projects/agent/.ddx/` (prefix `agent-*`) and will land as a single tagged point release; nothing in DDx lands until that release is pulled.

## Goals (unchanged — user stated 2026-04-22)

**G1. Zero-interaction defaults.** `ddx agent run` and `ddx agent execute-bead` just work with no flags.

**G2. Usable profiles.** `--profile standard`, `--profile local` force clear, documented rules.

**G3. Usable overrides.** `--harness claude` forces claude. `--model opus-4.7` forces opus-4.7. Within requirements: available, quota OK, lowest cost.

## Upstream dependencies (tracked in ~/Projects/agent/.ddx/)

This bead blocks on a single upstream point release that carries:

- Profile catalog with first-class `default`, `local`, `standard` (and any others DDx needs to expose) + documented provider-preference rules.
- Supported-models allow-list on routing-inputs `HarnessEntry`; gating rejects non-allowed models even when `ExactPinSupport: true`.
- Typed `ErrHarnessModelIncompatible` surfaced from `Execute` so explicit `--harness X --model Y` conflicts fail fast with actionable detail.
- Public `RouteDecision.Candidates` + typed error for "no viable candidate" with per-candidate rejection reasons — DDx's decision-trace consumers need this.
- Cost dimension on `ProviderEntry` (`CostUSDPer1kTokens`) + scoring tiebreak that prefers lower cost, with unknown-cost fallback, subscription-quota pricing, and near-quota demotion.
- Documentation for the profile catalog, override precedence, and "best provider" contract.

Once that release is tagged, DDx work below proceeds.

## DDx-side changes (reduced scope)

**D1. Dispatch sends a real spec.**

`workerDispatchAdapter.DispatchWorker` treats empty input as `{Profile: "default"}` explicitly. Dashboard Drain button calls the same path. No DDx-side default synthesis beyond "profile=default if unset."

**D2. Stop synthesizing a tier ladder by default.**

Default `ddx agent run` / `ddx agent execute-bead` / `ddx work` path calls `agentlib.ResolveRoute(Profile: spec.Profile)` with no Model string unless `spec.Model` was explicitly given. No per-tier iteration, no `ResolveTierModelRef` lookup, no preference-order fallback. The tier-ladder machinery becomes opt-in via `--escalate` only.

**D3. Preflight gate (defensive only, post-upstream).**

After `ResolveRoute` returns, DDx verifies the decision's `(Harness, Model)` is not in the typed-incompatibility set surfaced by upstream. The compatibility data comes from upstream — DDx does **not** duplicate the allow-list. Insertion point: `execute_bead_loop.go:352`, **before** `Claim`. Route/preflight failure exits the loop with a worker-level failure record; no bead is claimed, no burn cycle.

**D4. Deprecate DDx-side routing config.**

Deliberate breaking change in the same release that pulls the upstream point release:
- `agent.routing.default_harness` — field deleted; upstream's profile model makes it redundant.
- `agent.routing.profile_ladders` — kept but gated behind explicit `--escalate` only. Not read on the default path. Warning at config-load if set without `--escalate` ever being used in the life of the process.
- `agent.routing.model_overrides` — same treatment as `profile_ladders`: explicit-opt-in only, warning otherwise.
- `agent.harness` (top-level) — kept, clarified as a **preference for tie-breaks**, not a default override.

Migration note published in DDx changelog.

**D5. CLI surface parity with goals.**

- `ddx agent run` with zero flags → `ResolveRoute(Profile: "default")`. No other DDx inputs.
- `ddx agent run --profile local` → pass through unchanged to upstream.
- `ddx agent run --harness claude` → `ResolveRoute(Profile: "default", Harness: "claude")`. Upstream picks best provider within claude.
- `ddx agent run --model opus-4.7` → `ResolveRoute(Profile: "default", Model: "opus-4.7")`. Upstream picks lowest-cost available quota-OK provider.
- `ddx agent run --harness claude --model opus-4.7` → typed `ErrHarnessModelIncompatible` from upstream when incompatible; clean success otherwise.
- `ddx work` (drain) default → same as `ddx agent run` default.

## Explicitly dropped from this bead (per codex)

- **D6 — `ddx agent route-status` / providers UI decision-trace consumers.** Adjacent but not required. Files as a follow-up once A4 lands upstream. Hooks into `ddx-23978824` (providers UI).
- **D7 — `workersByProject` id/path filter.** Independent bug. Stays as `ddx-05b4cc9d`; not blocked by this bead and does not block this bead.

## Sequencing

1. **Upstream beads land and point release is tagged.** (Separate queue in `~/Projects/agent/.ddx/`.)
2. DDx `go.mod` bumps to that release.
3. D1, D2, D3, D5 land together in one DDx PR — no compensating paths retained.
4. D4 lands with D1-D3-D5 or in the immediate follow-up PR. Migration warning + changelog.
5. **Then** D6 / `ddx-23978824` / sessions-billing-mode (`ddx-6904a90b`) work can consume the new decision-trace surface.

## Cross-references

- Upstream beads: see `~/Projects/agent/.ddx/beads.jsonl` under label `routing-plan`.
- Direct codex review feedback logged in bead notes below.
- Supersedes original 3-step proposal from conversation history.
- Addresses `project_endpoint_routing_design.md` memory directive (2026-04-21): "kill named-provider profiles, move to endpoints + live discovery + fuzzy model match."
- Hard downstream dependents (wait for this bead):
  - `ddx-b6cf025c` — drain-worker count control (default profile must mean something first)
  - `ddx-23978824` — providers UI (needs decision trace from A4)
  - `ddx-6904a90b` — sessions billing-mode (benefits from richer session.routing metadata)
- Independent of this bead: `ddx-05b4cc9d` (workers filter).

## Codex review — key corrections folded in

1. A3 confirmed by code inspection: `HarnessEntry` has no model-allow-list field; `engine.go:456` `resolveModel` returns `req.Model` directly when no provider discovery; `gating.go:88` only checks `ExactPinSupport`. Allow-list must be added to routing inputs/capabilities, not just "fix ExactPin semantics."
2. A4 bigger than originally written: public `RouteDecision` has no `Candidates` field; service drops rejection data on error. Needs typed error + public surface.
3. Earlier claim that codex/claude have `Models: nil` was wrong — that's DDx's local `harness_config.go`, not upstream's `internal/harnesses/registry.go`. Upstream has codex=[gpt-5.4], claude=[sonnet, opus, claude-sonnet-4-6]. Allow-list work is universal upstream.
4. Upstream catalog has no `default` or `local` profile today (models.yaml:195). Must be added before DDx materializes them.
5. D3 restricted to defensive-only; all compatibility logic belongs upstream.
6. G2 precedence conflict (`--profile local --harness claude`): resolved in upstream docs as explicit-pin-wins but upstream returns typed error if the pin violates the profile's hard constraint (e.g., local-only with non-local harness).
7. G3 cost needs explicit data model — no price field on ProviderEntry today.
8. D4 reframed as deliberate breaking change, not warning-only drift.
9. D6 + D7 dropped from epic scope.
10. Quick-test gates added: upstream exact-pin regression; service-layer typed error preserves reasons; `ResolveRoute(Profile: "default")` against endpoint-only config; DDx worker exits before any bead claim.

.ddx/executions/20260429T152547-cea40d0b/prompt.md:5:## Status after codex review (2026-04-22)

Original plan was consolidated in this bead but **reduced in scope** after codex's review surfaced that most of the compatibility and data-model work belongs upstream in ddx-agent. This bead is now the **DDx-side tracker only**. Upstream work is queued as separate beads in `~/Projects/agent/.ddx/` (prefix `agent-*`) and will land as a single tagged point release; nothing in DDx lands until that release is pulled.

## Goals (unchanged — user stated 2026-04-22)

**G1. Zero-interaction defaults.** `ddx agent run` and `ddx agent execute-bead` just work with no flags.

**G2. Usable profiles.** `--profile standard`, `--profile local` force clear, documented rules.

**G3. Usable overrides.** `--harness claude` forces claude. `--model opus-4.7` forces opus-4.7. Within requirements: available, quota OK, lowest cost.

## Upstream dependencies (tracked in ~/Projects/agent/.ddx/)

This bead blocks on a single upstream point release that carries:

- Profile catalog with first-class `default`, `local`, `standard` (and any others DDx needs to expose) + documented provider-preference rules.
- Supported-models allow-list on routing-inputs `HarnessEntry`; gating rejects non-allowed models even when `ExactPinSupport: true`.
- Typed `ErrHarnessModelIncompatible` surfaced from `Execute` so explicit `--harness X --model Y` conflicts fail fast with actionable detail.
- Public `RouteDecision.Candidates` + typed error for "no viable candidate" with per-candidate rejection reasons — DDx's decision-trace consumers need this.
- Cost dimension on `ProviderEntry` (`CostUSDPer1kTokens`) + scoring tiebreak that prefers lower cost, with unknown-cost fallback, subscription-quota pricing, and near-quota demotion.
- Documentation for the profile catalog, override precedence, and "best provider" contract.

Once that release is tagged, DDx work below proceeds.

## DDx-side changes (reduced scope)

**D1. Dispatch sends a real spec.**

`workerDispatchAdapter.DispatchWorker` treats empty input as `{Profile: "default"}` explicitly. Dashboard Drain button calls the same path. No DDx-side default synthesis beyond "profile=default if unset."

**D2. Stop synthesizing a tier ladder by default.**

Default `ddx agent run` / `ddx agent execute-bead` / `ddx work` path calls `agentlib.ResolveRoute(Profile: spec.Profile)` with no Model string unless `spec.Model` was explicitly given. No per-tier iteration, no `ResolveTierModelRef` lookup, no preference-order fallback. The tier-ladder machinery becomes opt-in via `--escalate` only.

**D3. Preflight gate (defensive only, post-upstream).**

After `ResolveRoute` returns, DDx verifies the decision's `(Harness, Model)` is not in the typed-incompatibility set surfaced by upstream. The compatibility data comes from upstream — DDx does **not** duplicate the allow-list. Insertion point: `execute_bead_loop.go:352`, **before** `Claim`. Route/preflight failure exits the loop with a worker-level failure record; no bead is claimed, no burn cycle.

**D4. Deprecate DDx-side routing config.**

Deliberate breaking change in the same release that pulls the upstream point release:
- `agent.routing.default_harness` — field deleted; upstream's profile model makes it redundant.
- `agent.routing.profile_ladders` — kept but gated behind explicit `--escalate` only. Not read on the default path. Warning at config-load if set without `--escalate` ever being used in the life of the process.
- `agent.routing.model_overrides` — same treatment as `profile_ladders`: explicit-opt-in only, warning otherwise.
- `agent.harness` (top-level) — kept, clarified as a **preference for tie-breaks**, not a default override.

Migration note published in DDx changelog.

**D5. CLI surface parity with goals.**

- `ddx agent run` with zero flags → `ResolveRoute(Profile: "default")`. No other DDx inputs.
- `ddx agent run --profile local` → pass through unchanged to upstream.
- `ddx agent run --harness claude` → `ResolveRoute(Profile: "default", Harness: "claude")`. Upstream picks best provider within claude.
- `ddx agent run --model opus-4.7` → `ResolveRoute(Profile: "default", Model: "opus-4.7")`. Upstream picks lowest-cost available quota-OK provider.
- `ddx agent run --harness claude --model opus-4.7` → typed `ErrHarnessModelIncompatible` from upstream when incompatible; clean success otherwise.
- `ddx work` (drain) default → same as `ddx agent run` default.

## Explicitly dropped from this bead (per codex)

- **D6 — `ddx agent route-status` / providers UI decision-trace consumers.** Adjacent but not required. Files as a follow-up once A4 lands upstream. Hooks into `ddx-23978824` (providers UI).
- **D7 — `workersByProject` id/path filter.** Independent bug. Stays as `ddx-05b4cc9d`; not blocked by this bead and does not block this bead.

## Sequencing

1. **Upstream beads land and point release is tagged.** (Separate queue in `~/Projects/agent/.ddx/`.)
2. DDx `go.mod` bumps to that release.
3. D1, D2, D3, D5 land together in one DDx PR — no compensating paths retained.
4. D4 lands with D1-D3-D5 or in the immediate follow-up PR. Migration warning + changelog.
5. **Then** D6 / `ddx-23978824` / sessions-billing-mode (`ddx-6904a90b`) work can consume the new decision-trace surface.

## Cross-references

- Upstream beads: see `~/Projects/agent/.ddx/beads.jsonl` under label `routing-plan`.
- Direct codex review feedback logged in bead notes below.
- Supersedes original 3-step proposal from conversation history.
- Addresses `project_endpoint_routing_design.md` memory directive (2026-04-21): "kill named-provider profiles, move to endpoints + live discovery + fuzzy model match."
- Hard downstream dependents (wait for this bead):
  - `ddx-b6cf025c` — drain-worker count control (default profile must mean something first)
  - `ddx-23978824` — providers UI (needs decision trace from A4)
  - `ddx-6904a90b` — sessions billing-mode (benefits from richer session.routing metadata)
- Independent of this bead: `ddx-05b4cc9d` (workers filter).

## Codex review — key corrections folded in

1. A3 confirmed by code inspection: `HarnessEntry` has no model-allow-list field; `engine.go:456` `resolveModel` returns `req.Model` directly when no provider discovery; `gating.go:88` only checks `ExactPinSupport`. Allow-list must be added to routing inputs/capabilities, not just "fix ExactPin semantics."
2. A4 bigger than originally written: public `RouteDecision` has no `Candidates` field; service drops rejection data on error. Needs typed error + public surface.
3. Earlier claim that codex/claude have `Models: nil` was wrong — that's DDx's local `harness_config.go`, not upstream's `internal/harnesses/registry.go`. Upstream has codex=[gpt-5.4], claude=[sonnet, opus, claude-sonnet-4-6]. Allow-list work is universal upstream.
4. Upstream catalog has no `default` or `local` profile today (models.yaml:195). Must be added before DDx materializes them.
5. D3 restricted to defensive-only; all compatibility logic belongs upstream.
6. G2 precedence conflict (`--profile local --harness claude`): resolved in upstream docs as explicit-pin-wins but upstream returns typed error if the pin violates the profile's hard constraint (e.g., local-only with non-local harness).
7. G3 cost needs explicit data model — no price field on ProviderEntry today.
8. D4 reframed as deliberate breaking change, not warning-only drift.
9. D6 + D7 dropped from epic scope.
10. Quick-test gates added: upstream exact-pin regression; service-layer typed error preserves reasons; `ResolveRoute(Profile: "default")` against endpoint-only config; DDx worker exits before any bead claim.
.ddx/executions/20260414T231403-7acf4688/manifest.json:28:          "body": "Stage 2.2 (node/projects resolvers, commit 7e4ce6a) established the resolver pattern cleanly but left five decisions open that affect every subsequent resolver bead. Lock these in at Stage 2.3 (beads + beadsByProject) before the pattern propagates to 2.4-2.7.\n\n1. StateProvider interface split\n   Current: single StateProvider in graphql/resolver_node.go with 3 methods (GetNodeSnapshot/GetProjectSnapshots/GetProjectSnapshotByID). Stage 2.3 will add GetBeads/GetBeadByID/GetBeadsByProject — the interface will balloon to 50+ methods across all stages. Recommendation: split into per-resolver-group sub-interfaces (NodeState, BeadState, DocumentState, WorkerState, etc.) each defined next to its resolver file. The top-level Resolver.State field embeds a composite interface that combines them. File: introduce state_provider.go with the composite interface and move the existing StateProvider methods into a new NodeState interface.\n\n2. Stable-key cursors (Relay connections)\n   Current: resolver_node.go uses encodeCursor/decodeCursor that emit base64(\"cursor:\u003cslice-index\u003e\"). Slice-index cursors break under insert/delete between pages — fine for projects (rarely mutate) but broken for beads (mutated constantly). Stage 2.3 MUST use a stable-key cursor: base64 of (sort-key, id) or similar. Do not copy-paste the slice-index helpers from resolver_node.go; introduce stable-key helpers in a shared graphql/cursors.go and use them for beads/documents/commits/sessions.\n\n3. Snapshot struct duplication vs direct domain types\n   Current: graphql/resolver_node.go defines ProjectSnapshot that mirrors server.ProjectEntry field-for-field. Fine for 3 fields. Will become painful if every state type needs a shadow struct (Bead, Document, Worker, Session, ExecutionRun, Persona, Commit, ...). Decide now: either accept the snapshot pattern and live with duplication, OR let the graphql package depend on narrowly-factored state types directly. Pick ONE before Stage 2.3 so we do not end up with both patterns.\n\n4. r.State == nil nil-check\n   Current: resolver_node.go has if r.State == nil { return ...} guards in every method. Will copy-paste into 50 resolvers. Refactor: Resolver constructor rejects nil state, drop the runtime check. Trivial fix (one constructor), huge win in readability across 50 call sites.\n\n5. node(id) prefix dispatch\n   Current: node(id) switches on \"node-\"/\"proj-\" prefixes with an if-else chain. Stage 2.3 adds bead- prefix. Stage 2.4-2.7 add doc-, worker-, session-, exec-, persona- prefixes. The chain grows to 9 branches. Better: prefix-to-loader map (or a registry that each resolver file registers into from its init()). Lock this at 2.3 before the chain grows.\n\nNone of these are defects in 2.2. They are shape decisions that SHOULD be made at 2.3 to avoid technical debt accumulation across 2.4-2.7.",

.ddx/executions/20260428T032051-2dd22d56/prompt.md:5:## Status after codex review (2026-04-22)

Original plan was consolidated in this bead but **reduced in scope** after codex's review surfaced that most of the compatibility and data-model work belongs upstream in ddx-agent. This bead is now the **DDx-side tracker only**. Upstream work is queued as separate beads in `~/Projects/agent/.ddx/` (prefix `agent-*`) and will land as a single tagged point release; nothing in DDx lands until that release is pulled.

## Goals (unchanged — user stated 2026-04-22)

**G1. Zero-interaction defaults.** `ddx agent run` and `ddx agent execute-bead` just work with no flags.

**G2. Usable profiles.** `--profile standard`, `--profile local` force clear, documented rules.

**G3. Usable overrides.** `--harness claude` forces claude. `--model opus-4.7` forces opus-4.7. Within requirements: available, quota OK, lowest cost.

## Upstream dependencies (tracked in ~/Projects/agent/.ddx/)

This bead blocks on a single upstream point release that carries:

- Profile catalog with first-class `default`, `local`, `standard` (and any others DDx needs to expose) + documented provider-preference rules.
- Supported-models allow-list on routing-inputs `HarnessEntry`; gating rejects non-allowed models even when `ExactPinSupport: true`.
- Typed `ErrHarnessModelIncompatible` surfaced from `Execute` so explicit `--harness X --model Y` conflicts fail fast with actionable detail.
- Public `RouteDecision.Candidates` + typed error for "no viable candidate" with per-candidate rejection reasons — DDx's decision-trace consumers need this.
- Cost dimension on `ProviderEntry` (`CostUSDPer1kTokens`) + scoring tiebreak that prefers lower cost, with unknown-cost fallback, subscription-quota pricing, and near-quota demotion.
- Documentation for the profile catalog, override precedence, and "best provider" contract.

Once that release is tagged, DDx work below proceeds.

## DDx-side changes (reduced scope)

**D1. Dispatch sends a real spec.**

`workerDispatchAdapter.DispatchWorker` treats empty input as `{Profile: "default"}` explicitly. Dashboard Drain button calls the same path. No DDx-side default synthesis beyond "profile=default if unset."

**D2. Stop synthesizing a tier ladder by default.**

Default `ddx agent run` / `ddx agent execute-bead` / `ddx work` path calls `agentlib.ResolveRoute(Profile: spec.Profile)` with no Model string unless `spec.Model` was explicitly given. No per-tier iteration, no `ResolveTierModelRef` lookup, no preference-order fallback. The tier-ladder machinery becomes opt-in via `--escalate` only.

**D3. Preflight gate (defensive only, post-upstream).**

After `ResolveRoute` returns, DDx verifies the decision's `(Harness, Model)` is not in the typed-incompatibility set surfaced by upstream. The compatibility data comes from upstream — DDx does **not** duplicate the allow-list. Insertion point: `execute_bead_loop.go:352`, **before** `Claim`. Route/preflight failure exits the loop with a worker-level failure record; no bead is claimed, no burn cycle.

**D4. Deprecate DDx-side routing config.**

Deliberate breaking change in the same release that pulls the upstream point release:
- `agent.routing.default_harness` — field deleted; upstream's profile model makes it redundant.
- `agent.routing.profile_ladders` — kept but gated behind explicit `--escalate` only. Not read on the default path. Warning at config-load if set without `--escalate` ever being used in the life of the process.
- `agent.routing.model_overrides` — same treatment as `profile_ladders`: explicit-opt-in only, warning otherwise.
- `agent.harness` (top-level) — kept, clarified as a **preference for tie-breaks**, not a default override.

Migration note published in DDx changelog.

**D5. CLI surface parity with goals.**

- `ddx agent run` with zero flags → `ResolveRoute(Profile: "default")`. No other DDx inputs.
- `ddx agent run --profile local` → pass through unchanged to upstream.
- `ddx agent run --harness claude` → `ResolveRoute(Profile: "default", Harness: "claude")`. Upstream picks best provider within claude.
- `ddx agent run --model opus-4.7` → `ResolveRoute(Profile: "default", Model: "opus-4.7")`. Upstream picks lowest-cost available quota-OK provider.
- `ddx agent run --harness claude --model opus-4.7` → typed `ErrHarnessModelIncompatible` from upstream when incompatible; clean success otherwise.
- `ddx work` (drain) default → same as `ddx agent run` default.

## Explicitly dropped from this bead (per codex)

- **D6 — `ddx agent route-status` / providers UI decision-trace consumers.** Adjacent but not required. Files as a follow-up once A4 lands upstream. Hooks into `ddx-23978824` (providers UI).
- **D7 — `workersByProject` id/path filter.** Independent bug. Stays as `ddx-05b4cc9d`; not blocked by this bead and does not block this bead.

## Sequencing

1. **Upstream beads land and point release is tagged.** (Separate queue in `~/Projects/agent/.ddx/`.)
2. DDx `go.mod` bumps to that release.
3. D1, D2, D3, D5 land together in one DDx PR — no compensating paths retained.
4. D4 lands with D1-D3-D5 or in the immediate follow-up PR. Migration warning + changelog.
5. **Then** D6 / `ddx-23978824` / sessions-billing-mode (`ddx-6904a90b`) work can consume the new decision-trace surface.

## Cross-references

- Upstream beads: see `~/Projects/agent/.ddx/beads.jsonl` under label `routing-plan`.
- Direct codex review feedback logged in bead notes below.
- Supersedes original 3-step proposal from conversation history.
- Addresses `project_endpoint_routing_design.md` memory directive (2026-04-21): "kill named-provider profiles, move to endpoints + live discovery + fuzzy model match."
- Hard downstream dependents (wait for this bead):
  - `ddx-b6cf025c` — drain-worker count control (default profile must mean something first)
  - `ddx-23978824` — providers UI (needs decision trace from A4)
  - `ddx-6904a90b` — sessions billing-mode (benefits from richer session.routing metadata)
- Independent of this bead: `ddx-05b4cc9d` (workers filter).

## Codex review — key corrections folded in

1. A3 confirmed by code inspection: `HarnessEntry` has no model-allow-list field; `engine.go:456` `resolveModel` returns `req.Model` directly when no provider discovery; `gating.go:88` only checks `ExactPinSupport`. Allow-list must be added to routing inputs/capabilities, not just "fix ExactPin semantics."
2. A4 bigger than originally written: public `RouteDecision` has no `Candidates` field; service drops rejection data on error. Needs typed error + public surface.
3. Earlier claim that codex/claude have `Models: nil` was wrong — that's DDx's local `harness_config.go`, not upstream's `internal/harnesses/registry.go`. Upstream has codex=[gpt-5.4], claude=[sonnet, opus, claude-sonnet-4-6]. Allow-list work is universal upstream.
4. Upstream catalog has no `default` or `local` profile today (models.yaml:195). Must be added before DDx materializes them.
5. D3 restricted to defensive-only; all compatibility logic belongs upstream.
6. G2 precedence conflict (`--profile local --harness claude`): resolved in upstream docs as explicit-pin-wins but upstream returns typed error if the pin violates the profile's hard constraint (e.g., local-only with non-local harness).
7. G3 cost needs explicit data model — no price field on ProviderEntry today.
8. D4 reframed as deliberate breaking change, not warning-only drift.
9. D6 + D7 dropped from epic scope.
10. Quick-test gates added: upstream exact-pin regression; service-layer typed error preserves reasons; `ResolveRoute(Profile: "default")` against endpoint-only config; DDx worker exits before any bead claim.

.ddx/executions/20260428T222708-3b6ff345/prompt.md:5:## Status after codex review (2026-04-22)

Original plan was consolidated in this bead but **reduced in scope** after codex's review surfaced that most of the compatibility and data-model work belongs upstream in ddx-agent. This bead is now the **DDx-side tracker only**. Upstream work is queued as separate beads in `~/Projects/agent/.ddx/` (prefix `agent-*`) and will land as a single tagged point release; nothing in DDx lands until that release is pulled.

## Goals (unchanged — user stated 2026-04-22)

**G1. Zero-interaction defaults.** `ddx agent run` and `ddx agent execute-bead` just work with no flags.

**G2. Usable profiles.** `--profile standard`, `--profile local` force clear, documented rules.

**G3. Usable overrides.** `--harness claude` forces claude. `--model opus-4.7` forces opus-4.7. Within requirements: available, quota OK, lowest cost.

## Upstream dependencies (tracked in ~/Projects/agent/.ddx/)

This bead blocks on a single upstream point release that carries:

- Profile catalog with first-class `default`, `local`, `standard` (and any others DDx needs to expose) + documented provider-preference rules.
- Supported-models allow-list on routing-inputs `HarnessEntry`; gating rejects non-allowed models even when `ExactPinSupport: true`.
- Typed `ErrHarnessModelIncompatible` surfaced from `Execute` so explicit `--harness X --model Y` conflicts fail fast with actionable detail.
- Public `RouteDecision.Candidates` + typed error for "no viable candidate" with per-candidate rejection reasons — DDx's decision-trace consumers need this.
- Cost dimension on `ProviderEntry` (`CostUSDPer1kTokens`) + scoring tiebreak that prefers lower cost, with unknown-cost fallback, subscription-quota pricing, and near-quota demotion.
- Documentation for the profile catalog, override precedence, and "best provider" contract.

Once that release is tagged, DDx work below proceeds.

## DDx-side changes (reduced scope)

**D1. Dispatch sends a real spec.**

`workerDispatchAdapter.DispatchWorker` treats empty input as `{Profile: "default"}` explicitly. Dashboard Drain button calls the same path. No DDx-side default synthesis beyond "profile=default if unset."

**D2. Stop synthesizing a tier ladder by default.**

Default `ddx agent run` / `ddx agent execute-bead` / `ddx work` path calls `agentlib.ResolveRoute(Profile: spec.Profile)` with no Model string unless `spec.Model` was explicitly given. No per-tier iteration, no `ResolveTierModelRef` lookup, no preference-order fallback. The tier-ladder machinery becomes opt-in via `--escalate` only.

**D3. Preflight gate (defensive only, post-upstream).**

After `ResolveRoute` returns, DDx verifies the decision's `(Harness, Model)` is not in the typed-incompatibility set surfaced by upstream. The compatibility data comes from upstream — DDx does **not** duplicate the allow-list. Insertion point: `execute_bead_loop.go:352`, **before** `Claim`. Route/preflight failure exits the loop with a worker-level failure record; no bead is claimed, no burn cycle.

**D4. Deprecate DDx-side routing config.**

Deliberate breaking change in the same release that pulls the upstream point release:
- `agent.routing.default_harness` — field deleted; upstream's profile model makes it redundant.
- `agent.routing.profile_ladders` — kept but gated behind explicit `--escalate` only. Not read on the default path. Warning at config-load if set without `--escalate` ever being used in the life of the process.
- `agent.routing.model_overrides` — same treatment as `profile_ladders`: explicit-opt-in only, warning otherwise.
- `agent.harness` (top-level) — kept, clarified as a **preference for tie-breaks**, not a default override.

Migration note published in DDx changelog.

**D5. CLI surface parity with goals.**

- `ddx agent run` with zero flags → `ResolveRoute(Profile: "default")`. No other DDx inputs.
- `ddx agent run --profile local` → pass through unchanged to upstream.
- `ddx agent run --harness claude` → `ResolveRoute(Profile: "default", Harness: "claude")`. Upstream picks best provider within claude.
- `ddx agent run --model opus-4.7` → `ResolveRoute(Profile: "default", Model: "opus-4.7")`. Upstream picks lowest-cost available quota-OK provider.
- `ddx agent run --harness claude --model opus-4.7` → typed `ErrHarnessModelIncompatible` from upstream when incompatible; clean success otherwise.
- `ddx work` (drain) default → same as `ddx agent run` default.

## Explicitly dropped from this bead (per codex)

- **D6 — `ddx agent route-status` / providers UI decision-trace consumers.** Adjacent but not required. Files as a follow-up once A4 lands upstream. Hooks into `ddx-23978824` (providers UI).
- **D7 — `workersByProject` id/path filter.** Independent bug. Stays as `ddx-05b4cc9d`; not blocked by this bead and does not block this bead.

## Sequencing

1. **Upstream beads land and point release is tagged.** (Separate queue in `~/Projects/agent/.ddx/`.)
2. DDx `go.mod` bumps to that release.
3. D1, D2, D3, D5 land together in one DDx PR — no compensating paths retained.
4. D4 lands with D1-D3-D5 or in the immediate follow-up PR. Migration warning + changelog.
5. **Then** D6 / `ddx-23978824` / sessions-billing-mode (`ddx-6904a90b`) work can consume the new decision-trace surface.

## Cross-references

- Upstream beads: see `~/Projects/agent/.ddx/beads.jsonl` under label `routing-plan`.
- Direct codex review feedback logged in bead notes below.
- Supersedes original 3-step proposal from conversation history.
- Addresses `project_endpoint_routing_design.md` memory directive (2026-04-21): "kill named-provider profiles, move to endpoints + live discovery + fuzzy model match."
- Hard downstream dependents (wait for this bead):
  - `ddx-b6cf025c` — drain-worker count control (default profile must mean something first)
  - `ddx-23978824` — providers UI (needs decision trace from A4)
  - `ddx-6904a90b` — sessions billing-mode (benefits from richer session.routing metadata)
- Independent of this bead: `ddx-05b4cc9d` (workers filter).

## Codex review — key corrections folded in

1. A3 confirmed by code inspection: `HarnessEntry` has no model-allow-list field; `engine.go:456` `resolveModel` returns `req.Model` directly when no provider discovery; `gating.go:88` only checks `ExactPinSupport`. Allow-list must be added to routing inputs/capabilities, not just "fix ExactPin semantics."
2. A4 bigger than originally written: public `RouteDecision` has no `Candidates` field; service drops rejection data on error. Needs typed error + public surface.
3. Earlier claim that codex/claude have `Models: nil` was wrong — that's DDx's local `harness_config.go`, not upstream's `internal/harnesses/registry.go`. Upstream has codex=[gpt-5.4], claude=[sonnet, opus, claude-sonnet-4-6]. Allow-list work is universal upstream.
4. Upstream catalog has no `default` or `local` profile today (models.yaml:195). Must be added before DDx materializes them.
5. D3 restricted to defensive-only; all compatibility logic belongs upstream.
6. G2 precedence conflict (`--profile local --harness claude`): resolved in upstream docs as explicit-pin-wins but upstream returns typed error if the pin violates the profile's hard constraint (e.g., local-only with non-local harness).
7. G3 cost needs explicit data model — no price field on ProviderEntry today.
8. D4 reframed as deliberate breaking change, not warning-only drift.
9. D6 + D7 dropped from epic scope.
10. Quick-test gates added: upstream exact-pin regression; service-layer typed error preserves reasons; `ResolveRoute(Profile: "default")` against endpoint-only config; DDx worker exits before any bead claim.

## Upstream dependencies (tracked in ~/Projects/agent/.ddx/)

This bead blocks on a single upstream point release that carries:

- Profile catalog with first-class `default`, `local`, `standard` (and any others DDx needs to expose) + documented provider-preference rules.
- Supported-models allow-list on routing-inputs `HarnessEntry`; gating rejects non-allowed models even when `ExactPinSupport: true`.
- Typed `ErrHarnessModelIncompatible` surfaced from `Execute` so explicit `--harness X --model Y` conflicts fail fast with actionable detail.
- Public `RouteDecision.Candidates` + typed error for "no viable candidate" with per-candidate rejection reasons — DDx's decision-trace consumers need this.
- Cost dimension on `ProviderEntry` (`CostUSDPer1kTokens`) + scoring tiebreak that prefers lower cost, with unknown-cost fallback, subscription-quota pricing, and near-quota demotion.
- Documentation for the profile catalog, override precedence, and "best provider" contract.

Once that release is tagged, DDx work below proceeds.

## DDx-side changes (reduced scope)

**D1. Dispatch sends a real spec.**

`workerDispatchAdapter.DispatchWorker` treats empty input as `{Profile: "default"}` explicitly. Dashboard Drain button calls the same path. No DDx-side default synthesis beyond "profile=default if unset."

**D2. Stop synthesizing a tier ladder by default.**

Default `ddx agent run` / `ddx agent execute-bead` / `ddx work` path calls `agentlib.ResolveRoute(Profile: spec.Profile)` with no Model string unless `spec.Model` was explicitly given. No per-tier iteration, no `ResolveTierModelRef` lookup, no preference-order fallback. The tier-ladder machinery becomes opt-in via `--escalate` only.

**D3. Preflight gate (defensive only, post-upstream).**

After `ResolveRoute` returns, DDx verifies the decision's `(Harness, Model)` is not in the typed-incompatibility set surfaced by upstream. The compatibility data comes from upstream — DDx does **not** duplicate the allow-list. Insertion point: `execute_bead_loop.go:352`, **before** `Claim`. Route/preflight failure exits the loop with a worker-level failure record; no bead is claimed, no burn cycle.

**D4. Deprecate DDx-side routing config.**

Deliberate breaking change in the same release that pulls the upstream point release:
- `agent.routing.default_harness` — field deleted; upstream's profile model makes it redundant.
- `agent.routing.profile_ladders` — kept but gated behind explicit `--escalate` only. Not read on the default path. Warning at config-load if set without `--escalate` ever being used in the life of the process.
- `agent.routing.model_overrides` — same treatment as `profile_ladders`: explicit-opt-in only, warning otherwise.
- `agent.harness` (top-level) — kept, clarified as a **preference for tie-breaks**, not a default override.

Migration note published in DDx changelog.

**D5. CLI surface parity with goals.**

- `ddx agent run` with zero flags → `ResolveRoute(Profile: "default")`. No other DDx inputs.
- `ddx agent run --profile local` → pass through unchanged to upstream.
- `ddx agent run --harness claude` → `ResolveRoute(Profile: "default", Harness: "claude")`. Upstream picks best provider within claude.
- `ddx agent run --model opus-4.7` → `ResolveRoute(Profile: "default", Model: "opus-4.7")`. Upstream picks lowest-cost available quota-OK provider.
- `ddx agent run --harness claude --model opus-4.7` → typed `ErrHarnessModelIncompatible` from upstream when incompatible; clean success otherwise.
- `ddx work` (drain) default → same as `ddx agent run` default.

## Explicitly dropped from this bead (per codex)

- **D6 — `ddx agent route-status` / providers UI decision-trace consumers.** Adjacent but not required. Files as a follow-up once A4 lands upstream. Hooks into `ddx-23978824` (providers UI).
- **D7 — `workersByProject` id/path filter.** Independent bug. Stays as `ddx-05b4cc9d`; not blocked by this bead and does not block this bead.

## Sequencing

1. **Upstream beads land and point release is tagged.** (Separate queue in `~/Projects/agent/.ddx/`.)
2. DDx `go.mod` bumps to that release.
3. D1, D2, D3, D5 land together in one DDx PR — no compensating paths retained.
4. D4 lands with D1-D3-D5 or in the immediate follow-up PR. Migration warning + changelog.
5. **Then** D6 / `ddx-23978824` / sessions-billing-mode (`ddx-6904a90b`) work can consume the new decision-trace surface.

## Cross-references

- Upstream beads: see `~/Projects/agent/.ddx/beads.jsonl` under label `routing-plan`.
- Direct codex review feedback logged in bead notes below.
- Supersedes original 3-step proposal from conversation history.
- Addresses `project_endpoint_routing_design.md` memory directive (2026-04-21): "kill named-provider profiles, move to endpoints + live discovery + fuzzy model match."
- Hard downstream dependents (wait for this bead):
  - `ddx-b6cf025c` — drain-worker count control (default profile must mean something first)
  - `ddx-23978824` — providers UI (needs decision trace from A4)
  - `ddx-6904a90b` — sessions billing-mode (benefits from richer session.routing metadata)
- Independent of this bead: `ddx-05b4cc9d` (workers filter).

## Codex review — key corrections folded in

1. A3 confirmed by code inspection: `HarnessEntry` has no model-allow-list field; `engine.go:456` `resolveModel` returns `req.Model` directly when no provider discovery; `gating.go:88` only checks `ExactPinSupport`. Allow-list must be added to routing inputs/capabilities, not just "fix ExactPin semantics."
2. A4 bigger than originally written: public `RouteDecision` has no `Candidates` field; service drops rejection data on error. Needs typed error + public surface.
3. Earlier claim that codex/claude have `Models: nil` was wrong — that's DDx's local `harness_config.go`, not upstream's `internal/harnesses/registry.go`. Upstream has codex=[gpt-5.4], claude=[sonnet, opus, claude-sonnet-4-6]. Allow-list work is universal upstream.
4. Upstream catalog has no `default` or `local` profile today (models.yaml:195). Must be added before DDx materializes them.
5. D3 restricted to defensive-only; all compatibility logic belongs upstream.
6. G2 precedence conflict (`--profile local --harness claude`): resolved in upstream docs as explicit-pin-wins but upstream returns typed error if the pin violates the profile's hard constraint (e.g., local-only with non-local harness).
7. G3 cost needs explicit data model — no price field on ProviderEntry today.
8. D4 reframed as deliberate breaking change, not warning-only drift.
9. D6 + D7 dropped from epic scope.
10. Quick-test gates added: upstream exact-pin regression; service-layer typed error preserves reasons; `ResolveRoute(Profile: "default")` against endpoint-only config; DDx worker exits before any bead claim.

.ddx/executions/20260425T090348-65bbbbb8/prompt.md:5:Stage A2 of FEAT-022. Implements the repo-wide lint rule enforcing
the no-unbounded-prompts invariant (FEAT-022 §3 + Non-Functional §Lint).
Satisfies US-226.

Depends on Stage A1 (ddx-64299657) — needs the evidence-package
primitives to exist so the lint rule can whitelist them.

Tooling choice (decided, no worker ambiguity): implement as a Go
analyzer under cli/tools/lint/evidencelint/ runnable via
`go run ./tools/lint/evidencelint/cmd/evidencelint ./...` and wired
into Lefthook pre-commit as an additional check. This is the simplest
path that works with the existing golangci-lint + go-vet stack and
avoids a custom golangci-lint plugin build. If this choice proves
wrong, revisit in a follow-up bead — do not expand scope here.

The analyzer flags, and requires either routing through evidence.* or
an `evidence:allow-unbounded reason="..."` comment on:
- Any assignment expression whose LHS is a selector matching
  `*.Prompt` where the receiver type is the agent RunOptions struct.
- Any composite-literal construction of `mcpContent` with `Type: "text"`.
- Any call to `os.ReadFile` / `ioutil.ReadFile` whose result flows
  into a variable named `*prompt*` or `*Prompt*` (structural heuristic
  covering the identified ingress sites).
- Any HTTP handler function writing response bytes that it obtained
  from library-doc, execution-bundle, diff, session, or persona
  sources (detected structurally by the source function's package
  prefix, not general dataflow).

Static/literal prompt fragments (constant string expressions compiled
into the binary) are exempt.

In-scope files:
- cli/tools/lint/evidencelint/ (new): analyzer.go, analyzer_test.go,
  and cmd/evidencelint/main.go entry point.
- lefthook.yml (existing): add evidence-lint pre-commit hook.
- .github/workflows/ CI config: add evidence-lint job or fold into
  existing lint job.

Out-of-scope:
- Any existing prompt-building call site. This bead only adds the
  analyzer. Existing violations are expected and tolerated via
  `evidence:allow-unbounded` annotations added in subsequent stages
  (B–E) as those stages migrate each site.
- Dataflow analysis. Structural coverage only — per FEAT-022
  Non-Functional §Lint.

Rollback: remove cli/tools/lint/evidencelint/, revert lefthook.yml
and CI workflow edits.
.ddx/executions/20260415T042450-19120590/manifest.json:14:    "description": "Follow-up from Stage 2.4 opus review: the resolver pattern established in Stage 2.2 (node/projects) and extended in Stage 2.4 (documents/docGraph) has 3 load-bearing shape issues that should be fixed before Stage 2.5+ compounds them across 7 more resolver beads.\n\n1. Stable-key cursors. Current: resolver_node.go defines encodeCursor(i int) / decodeCursor(s string) that base64-wrap \"cursor:\u003cslice-index\u003e\". Slice-index cursors break when the underlying collection reorders between pages. Fine for projects (rarely mutate) but broken for beads, documents, commits, sessions, workers (mutated or resorted per query). Replace with ID-based or (sort-key, ID)-based stable cursors. New file: cli/internal/server/graphql/cursors.go with encodeStableCursor(id string) / decodeStableCursor(s string).\n\n2. Constructor-enforced non-nil state. Current: every resolver has \"if r.State == nil { return }\" or \"if r.WorkingDir == empty { return }\" early returns. As Stage 2 grows this becomes 50+ copy-pasted guards. Replace with a NewResolver(state StateProvider, workingDir string) *Resolver constructor that rejects nil/empty and returns an error, drop the runtime checks.\n\n3. Snapshot struct vs direct domain types. Current inconsistency: resolver_node.go uses ProjectSnapshot / NodeStateSnapshot (adapter structs) while resolver_documents.go uses docgraph.Document directly. Pick ONE approach and migrate the other. Recommendation: direct domain types (simpler, less boilerplate). Migrate resolver_node.go to drop ProjectSnapshot.\n\nNot in scope: the prefix-registry for node(id) dispatch (concern 5 from the Stage 2.3 note) — keep that for when bead/doc/worker prefixes are added. Not in scope: splitting StateProvider into sub-interfaces (concern 1) — decide that lazily when the interface actually grows.\n\nFile: cli/internal/server/graphql/{cursors.go,resolver.go,resolver_node.go,resolver_documents.go,state_graphql.go}",

The bead- prefix is not handled. That test passes trivially on any cursor implementation including broken slice-index.\nRequired fix: create \u003e= 10 beads, query first 5, mutate the dataset (delete 1 bead at index 2, create 1 new bead), query next page via the cursor from step 1, assert no duplicates AND no skipped beads. .ddx/executions/20260415T042450-19120590/prompt.md:5:Follow-up from Stage 2.4 opus review: the resolver pattern established in Stage 2.2 (node/projects) and extended in Stage 2.4 (documents/docGraph) has 3 load-bearing shape issues that should be fixed before Stage 2.5+ compounds them across 7 more resolver beads.

1. Stable-key cursors. Current: resolver_node.go defines encodeCursor(i int) / decodeCursor(s string) that base64-wrap "cursor:<slice-index>". Slice-index cursors break when the underlying collection reorders between pages. Fine for projects (rarely mutate) but broken for beads, documents, commits, sessions, workers (mutated or resorted per query). Replace with ID-based or (sort-key, ID)-based stable cursors. New file: cli/internal/server/graphql/cursors.go with encodeStableCursor(id string) / decodeStableCursor(s string).

2. Constructor-enforced non-nil state. Current: every resolver has "if r.State == nil { return }" or "if r.WorkingDir == empty { return }" early returns. As Stage 2 grows this becomes 50+ copy-pasted guards. Replace with a NewResolver(state StateProvider, workingDir string) *Resolver constructor that rejects nil/empty and returns an error, drop the runtime checks.

3. Snapshot struct vs direct domain types. Current inconsistency: resolver_node.go uses ProjectSnapshot / NodeStateSnapshot (adapter structs) while resolver_documents.go uses docgraph.Document directly. Pick ONE approach and migrate the other. Recommendation: direct domain types (simpler, less boilerplate). Migrate resolver_node.go to drop ProjectSnapshot.

Not in scope: the prefix-registry for node(id) dispatch (concern 5 from the Stage 2.3 note) — keep that for when bead/doc/worker prefixes are added. Not in scope: splitting StateProvider into sub-interfaces (concern 1) — decide that lazily when the interface actually grows.

File: cli/internal/server/graphql/{cursors.go,resolver.go,resolver_node.go,resolver_documents.go,state_graphql.go}

.ddx/executions/20260423T045613-389c0053/prompt.md:5:## Observed

The Sessions page ($ sums `session.cost` into one "Total Cost" card, `cli/internal/server/frontend/src/routes/.../sessions/+page.svelte:21,28,91`). The underlying `cost_usd` field is fed from three very different billing realities and the UI treats them as one:

1. **Cash paid (pay-per-token).** API providers (OpenRouter, direct OpenAI, direct Anthropic API). `cost_usd` = actual billed amount.
2. **Subscription-equivalent.** Claude Code, Codex — flat-rate subscriptions. The CLI/harness emits `total_cost_usd` as the **dollar-equivalent** for tokens consumed under the subscription (what you would have paid pay-per-token). You paid a flat fee up front; this is a value-extracted number, not a cash-out number.
3. **Local compute.** Embedded `agent` harness, ollama/vllm endpoints. Today emits cost 0 because no one is billing; real cost is electricity + hardware amortization, not currently modeled.

A user reading "Total Cost: $X" does not know which of those X is.

## What's needed

Three independent rollups, each explicitly labeled, each computable without ambiguity:

1. **Cash** — sum of `cost_usd` across sessions whose billing mode is `paid`.
2. **Subscription-equivalent** — sum across sessions whose billing mode is `subscription`. Copy: "Dollar-equivalent for tokens consumed via Claude Code / Codex subscription. You did not pay this in cash; it's the pay-per-token rate you would have been charged."
3. **Local** — either a count of sessions (if no rate is configured) or a computed dollar estimate if `local_cost_per_1k_tokens` is set in config. Default: count-only, to avoid inventing numbers.

## Backing data model

Session records (the sharded index from `ddx-2ceb02fa`) need a new field `billingMode: "paid" | "subscription" | "local"`. Derive at write time:

- `paid`: harness is an API-endpoint provider (openrouter, openai-compat with an API key, anthropic API, …). Probably any harness with `Surface == "openai-compat"` or a known public-api provider prefix.
- `subscription`: harness is `claude` (Claude Code) or `codex` or `gemini-cli` when using a Google account. Identify by harness name + surface.
- `local`: harness is `agent` (embedded) OR endpoint is localhost / private IP / configured-as-local in provider config.

Classification lives in one place — a small `billingModeFor(harness, surface, baseURL)` function — so the logic isn't duplicated between writer and reader.

## Scope

### Part 1 — Data model

- Extend `SessionEntry` / the session index row with `billingMode string` (enum validated on write).
- Add `billingModeFor(...)` helper + unit tests for each harness/surface combination.
- Back-fill: `ddx agent log reindex` (from `ddx-2ceb02fa`) computes `billingMode` when re-shard-writing legacy sessions.

### Part 2 — Backend rollup

- Modify the sessions GraphQL query (or add a sibling `sessionsCostSummary(projectId, since?)`) to return `{ cashUsd, subscriptionEquivUsd, localSessionCount, localEstimatedUsd? }`.
- Reuse the perf-safe path from `ddx-0a33bc5f` (sessions-index aggregation, shard-aware date filter).

### Part 3 — UI

- Replace the single "Total Cost" card on the Sessions page with three cards:
  - **Cash paid** ($X.XX) — tooltip "Billed by pay-per-token APIs (OpenRouter, direct API keys)"
  - **Subscription value** ($X.XX) — tooltip "Dollar-equivalent for tokens consumed under Claude Code / Codex subscriptions. Not cash out of pocket."
  - **Local sessions** (N) — tooltip "Sessions served locally. Compute cost not modeled." If `local_cost_per_1k_tokens` is set, show "$X.XX est." instead of a count.
- Per-row: small badge `cash` / `sub` / `local` in the cost column, color-coded (green/blue/gray). Tooltip explains the row's bucket.

### Part 4 — Optional config

- `.ddx/config.yaml` gains `cost.local_per_1k_tokens: <float>`. When set, local-session total gets rendered as dollars. When unset, count-only. No auto-estimation.

## Out of scope

- Subscription flat-rate cost modeling ("I pay $20/month for Claude Code"). A useful adjacent bead: divide flat rate by consumption to show $/1M-tokens realized value. Not here.
- Quota trend / headroom. Covered by `ddx-23978824`.
- Reclassifying historical sessions beyond what reindex can infer from their stored harness/surface fields.

Dry-run prints the summary without writing.\n   - Integration test uses a fixture state containing 10 real + 100 fake entries, runs the command, asserts 10 remain and a backup file with exactly 110 entries was written.\n\n4. **Perf amplifier — regression test.**\n   - A test asserts `GetBeadSnapshots` does **not** call `os.Stat` on any project already tombstoned or pattern-filtered. The sweep upgrade is safe on restart — no data loss for real projects.\n   - On upgrade, a note is logged: \"Pruned X phantom test-dir projects from state file.\"\n\n6. **Cross-reference.**\n   - Bead notes link to `ddx-9ce6842a` (beads perf) and `ddx-2ceb02fa` (sessions feed) as the two surfaces whose perf problems are amplified by this bug. Those beads' fixture sizing assumptions were correct for a clean state file; the operator experience will still feel slow on a polluted machine until Fix C is run.",
.ddx/executions/20260423T040113-a89a35ab/prompt.md:8:**User story:** As a developer running `go test`, my test runs do not leave behind registered projects in my real `~/.local/share/ddx/server-state.json`. When a machine has already been polluted, I can prune it with one command. The server sweep is aggressive enough that obvious test-dir pollution does not survive a restart.

**Acceptance criteria:**

1. **Fix A — test isolation enforced by CI.**
   - Every existing test that instantiates `ServerState` or boots a server helper is migrated to an isolated state dir. No test writes to `~/.local/share/ddx/server-state.json`.
   - A lint test (Go or shell) parses `cli/internal/server/**/*_test.go` (and any other directories that wire up `ServerState`) and fails if it finds a construction that does not pass an explicit isolated state path or override `XDG_DATA_HOME` first. Test cases added for both the allowed-pattern and the disallowed-pattern.

2. **Fix B — sweep recognizes test-dir pollution.**
   - `ServerState.migrate` drops entries whose `Path` matches any of:
     - prefix `/tmp/`, `/private/tmp/`, `/var/folders/`
     - regex `/Test[A-Z][A-Za-z0-9_]*\d+/` anywhere in the path
   - Missing paths matching these patterns are dropped on first sweep (no 24h tombstone).
   - Reachable paths matching these patterns are still dropped (they are never real projects — the test dir may exist transiently but is never the user's registered project).
   - Unit test seeds a fixture state with one real project, one `/tmp/TestFoo123/001` missing, one `/tmp/TestBar456/002` still on disk, and one `/private/tmp/TestMac789/001`; asserts only the real project survives.

3. **Fix C — pruning CLI.**
   - `ddx server state prune [--dry-run] [--state <path>]` applies the Fix B rules to an on-disk state file.
   - Writes `server-state.json.bak-<YYYYMMDDTHHMMSS>` before overwriting.
   - Prints summary: `Pruned X of Y entries (Z kept)`. Dry-run prints the summary without writing.
   - Integration test uses a fixture state containing 10 real + 100 fake entries, runs the command, asserts 10 remain and a backup file with exactly 110 entries was written.

4. **Perf amplifier — regression test.**
   - A test asserts `GetBeadSnapshots` does **not** call `os.Stat` on any project already tombstoned or pattern-filtered. (Implementation detail: sweep on startup + skip tombstoned in the hot loop.)
   - If Fix A+B are in place, a fresh `go test ./...` run followed by a server restart produces a state file with ≤ 20 projects on a typical dev machine. Asserted via a CI job that runs the tests, starts the server briefly, inspects the state file, and fails if test-dir patterns are present.

5. **Backward compatibility.**
   - The prune CLI is safe to run on an existing polluted state file. The sweep upgrade is safe on restart — no data loss for real projects.
   - On upgrade, a note is logged: "Pruned X phantom test-dir projects from state file."

6. **Cross-reference.**
   - Bead notes link to `ddx-9ce6842a` (beads perf) and `ddx-2ceb02fa` (sessions feed) as the two surfaces whose perf problems are amplified by this bug. Those beads' fixture sizing assumptions were correct for a clean state file; the operator experience will still feel slow on a polluted machine until Fix C is run.
